extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system exemplars demonstrate a **decentralized, ad-hoc architecture** with **single-instance deployments** per use case (e.g., GreenhouseDT, Air Quality Management, and the lab-scale gantry crane). No explicit mention of **multiple digital twin instances** or **hierarchical coordination** is provided. The GreenhouseDT exemplar uses **virtual machines with prerecorded data**, suggesting a **static, offline-capable synchronization** mechanism, while the Air Quality Management exemplar relies on **real-time sensor data (Raspberry Pi + sensors)** for runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction, implying **near-real-time synchronization** (latency unspecified). The gantry crane system lacks explicit temporal granularity details but is described using **C4 Model architecture** (software-centric), implying modular but not inherently distributed time-scale management. **No centralized coordination protocol** (e.g., consensus algorithms, event-driven buses) is documented beyond **ActiveMQ (GreenhouseDT)** and **Microsoft Azure tools (Air Quality Management)**, which may infer **message-broker-based synchronization** but without defined frequencies or SLAs.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system exemplars employ a **lightweight, ad-hoc orchestration pattern** without a unified framework. **GreenhouseDT** uses **InfluxDB (time-series database) + ActiveMQ (message broker) + virtual machines**, suggesting a **service-oriented architecture (SOA)** with **loose coupling** between physical (greenhouse) and digital components. **Air Quality Management** leverages **AutomationML-based digital twin modeling** and **Microsoft Azure tools** (unspecified services) for a **pipeline-driven orchestration**, integrating **Raspberry Pi sensor data** via **containerized or virtualized deployment**. The **gantry crane system** (logistics/cyber-physical domain) adopts **C4 Model architecture** (context/container diagrams), implying **modular component integration** but no explicit orchestration framework. **Resource management** is implied via **virtualization/containerization** (e.g., VMs, Raspberry Pi) and **prerecorded data** (GreenhouseDT), while **system-wide coordination** relies on **ad-hoc message passing** (ActiveMQ) or **cloud-native pipelines** (Azure). **No global state management** or **federated twin coordination** is described.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin exemplars exhibit **limited horizontal integration** with external systems, relying on **domain-specific, ad-hoc protocols**. **GreenhouseDT** integrates with **InfluxDB (time-series storage)** and **ActiveMQ (message broker)**, but no interoperability standards (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) or enterprise system dependencies are specified. **Air Quality Management** uses **AutomationML** for digital twin modeling and **Microsoft Azure tools** (unspecified APIs/services), suggesting **cloud-based data exchange** but no explicit standard. The **gantry crane system** lacks external integration details beyond its **C4 Model architecture**, which is software-centric. **No mention of integration patterns** (e.g., APIs, microservices, or Digital Twin interoperability frameworks like **DMTF’s Open Digital Twin standards**) is provided. Dependencies are **internal to the exemplar’s tech stack** (e.g., Raspberry Pi sensors, VMs, Azure), with **no cross-system coordination** (e.g., with other Digital Twins or ERP/MES systems).
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system leverages **AutomationML-based digital twin models** for the Air Quality Management exemplar, enabling structured representation of physical and logical components in a standardized format. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar employs **behavioral adaptation models** for both the physical greenhouse and its digital twin, with **prerecorded data** embedded in virtual machine images for offline simulation and testing. The system integrates **InfluxDB** for time-series data storage and **ActiveMQ** as a message broker for real-time data exchange between physical and digital components. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar includes **runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction models**, implemented using **Microsoft Azure tools** for cloud-based digital twin pipeline execution. Model relationships are implicitly defined through **sensor-actuator feedback loops** and **data-driven adaptation mechanisms**, where physical sensor data informs model updates in real-time. The **C4 Model** is used for architectural visualization, though it is primarily tailored for software systems rather than explicit model relationships in the Digital Twin constellation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The **Air Quality Management** exemplar utilizes a **Raspberry Pi** equipped with a **set of sensors** for real-time environmental monitoring, including parameters relevant to indoor air quality (e.g., CO₂, particulate matter, temperature, humidity). The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar does not explicitly detail sensor types but relies on **physical system data collection** (implied by behavioral adaptation) via unspecified sensors interfaced with the **InfluxDB** database. The **sampling frequencies** and **data transmission rates** are not specified in the documents, though the use of **ActiveMQ** suggests low-latency message brokering for sensor data. The **spatial distribution** of sensors is not detailed, but the Raspberry Pi’s modularity implies localized deployment. **Accuracy and precision specifications** are not provided, nor are explicit **data collection mechanisms** beyond sensor-to-database integration.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar emphasizes **self-adaptive behavioral models** with **prerecorded data** in virtual machines, implying a focus on **model fidelity through real-time adaptation** of both physical and digital systems. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar employs **runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction** models, suggesting **validation via comparative analysis** between simulated and real-world air quality metrics. **Uncertainty sources** are not explicitly quantified, but the use of **AutomationML** and **Microsoft Azure tools** implies structured model validation pipelines. **Error detection and correction mechanisms** are inferred through the **adaptive feedback loops** in GreenhouseDT and the **Azure-based digital twin pipeline** in Air Quality Management, though no specific algorithms or thresholds are mentioned. **Quality assurance processes** are not detailed beyond the use of **containerization/virtualization** for reproducible deployments and **GitHub-hosted documentation** for transparency.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in the lab-scale gantry crane Digital Twin system involves real-time data transmission from the **Physical Crane** to the **Digital Twin** components via the **Physical Crane Interface** (a Python program). The data flows include:
- **Data Types Transmitted**:
  - Crane state data (e.g., position, velocity, acceleration of the gantry, puck, and payload).
  - Controller telemetry (e.g., motor currents, encoder readings, and sensor feedback).
  - Trajectory execution metrics (e.g., timestamps, waypoints, and residual motion measurements).
- **Communication Protocol**: Implicitly inferred as **MQTT** (via the **MQTT Broker** used for inter-service signaling) or direct Python-based communication (e.g., TCP/IP sockets or serial interfaces for controller integration).
- **Transmission Frequencies**: Not explicitly stated, but inferred to be **high-frequency** (e.g., ≥10Hz) to capture dynamic crane motion and ensure trajectory validation accuracy.
- **Event Triggers**:
  - Continuous logging of crane state by the **Logging Application** (asynchronous, time-based).
  - Trajectory execution events (e.g., start/stop of movement, waypoint completion) triggering data ingestion by the **Trajectory Generator Service** and **Simulation Service**.
- **Data Quality Assurance**:
  - Preprocessing in the **Logging Application** (e.g., filtering noise, timestamp synchronization).
  - Validation by the **Continuous Validation Service**, which compares real-time measurements against simulated trajectories using **distance metrics** (e.g., Euclidean or dynamic time warping) and thresholds.
- **Data Integration Mechanisms**:
  - Centralized storage in a **TimescaleDB** database (optimized for time-series data).
  - Real-time ingestion via the **MQTT Broker** for low-latency service communication.
  - Grafana **Dashboard** for visualization and operator monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in the Digital Twin system is mediated by the **Control Application** and **Trajectory Generator Service**, which translate digital commands into physical actions on the **Physical Crane**. Key aspects include:
- **Command Types**:
  - **Trajectory commands**: Optimal movement paths generated by the **Trajectory Generator Service** (kinematics-based, ensuring zero residual swinging at waypoints).
  - **Configuration commands**: Sent via the **Human Machine Interface (HMI)** to adjust crane parameters (e.g., speed limits, safety thresholds).
  - **Simulation-derived corrections**: Adjustments to trajectories based on **Continuous Validation Service** feedback (e.g., compensating for unmodeled dynamics).
- **Control Message Formats**:
  - Structured data (e.g., JSON or protobuf) for trajectory waypoints (coordinates, timestamps, velocity profiles).
  - Binary or serialized Python objects for low-level controller commands (e.g., PWM signals for motors).
- **Validation and Authentication Mechanisms**:
  - **Threshold-based validation**: The **Continuous Validation Service** compares executed trajectories to simulations; breaches trigger alerts via the **Dashboard**.
  - **Implicit authentication**: Assumed via containerized service isolation (Python applications running in trusted environments) and MQTT topic-based access control.
- **Feedback Systems**:
  - **Closed-loop control**: The **Control Application** receives real-time sensor data from the **Physical Crane Interface** to adjust commands dynamically.
  - **Operator feedback**: Alerts in the **Dashboard** notify of deviations (e.g., trajectory errors exceeding thresholds).
- **Conflict Resolution**:
  - Priority-based: Trajectory commands override manual HMI inputs during automated operations.
  - Fail-safes: The **Control Application** halts motion if sensor data indicates unsafe conditions (e.g., puck detachment).
- **Command Execution Confirmation**:
  - Acknowledgment via **MQTT messages** or database logs (e.g., timestamped records of executed trajectories in TimescaleDB).
  - Visual confirmation in the **Dashboard** (e.g., real-time crane state overlay on simulated trajectories).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin’s technical network infrastructure** relies on a **service-oriented architecture** with the following specifications:
- **Communication Protocols**:
  - **MQTT**: Used for lightweight, publish-subscribe messaging between services (e.g., **Trajectory Generator Service** → **Control Application**).
  - **Python-based APIs**: Direct inter-process communication (IPC) between containers (e.g., **Physical Crane Interface** ↔ **Control Application**).
  - **TimescaleDB protocol**: Likely PostgreSQL-compatible (TimescaleDB is an extension) for structured data storage and querying.
- **Network Topology and Architecture**:
  - **Containerized microservices**: Each Digital Twin component (e.g., **Trajectory Generator Service**, **Simulation Service**) runs as an isolated container, communicating via the **MQTT Broker** or direct Python calls.
  - **Centralized database**: **TimescaleDB** acts as a single source of truth for all time-series data.
- **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
  - **Low-latency**: Critical for trajectory validation (e.g., <100ms round-trip for control loops).
  - **Moderate bandwidth**: Assumed sufficient for sensor data (e.g., 10–100Hz updates) and trajectory commands.
- **Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**:
  - **Database replication**: TimescaleDB supports high availability (not explicitly detailed but implied for production use).
  - **Service resilience**: Containerized deployment allows for restarts or scaling of failed services (e.g., **MQTT Broker** or **Logging Application**).
- **Security Protocols**:
  - **MQTT over TLS**: Assumed for encrypted broker communication (not explicitly stated but standard practice).
  - **Container isolation**: Services run in separate containers with minimal exposed interfaces.
  - **Database security**: TimescaleDB likely enforces role-based access control (RBAC) for data integrity.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin’s hosting and deployment infrastructure** is designed for **local/edge deployment** with the following technical details:
- **Hosting Platforms**:
  - **On-premise/edge**: The system is tailored for the lab-scale gantry crane environment, implying deployment on local hardware (e.g., a Raspberry Pi cluster or industrial PC) rather than cloud.
  - **Containerization**: All services (e.g., **Trajectory Generator Service**, **Simulation Service**) are deployed as Python applications in isolated containers (likely using Docker or similar).
- **Computational and Storage Requirements**:
  - **CPU**: Moderate requirements for kinematics simulations and real-time trajectory generation (e.g., multi-core for parallel processing of trajectories).
  - **Memory**: Sufficient for running multiple Python services concurrently (e.g., 4–8GB RAM for the entire stack).
  - **Storage**: **TimescaleDB** requires persistent storage for time-series data (e.g., SSD for low-latency writes).
- **Scalability Mechanisms**:
  - **Horizontal scaling**: Additional instances of stateless services (e.g., **Simulation Service**) can be deployed for parallel trajectory simulations.
  - **Database partitioning**: TimescaleDB supports hypertables for automatic data distribution across disks.
- **Deployment Models Supported**:
  - **Monolithic containerization**: All Digital Twin services are co-located with the physical system (no cloud dependency).
  - **Hybrid potential**: Could extend to cloud for analytics (e.g., offloading historical data to AWS/Azure), but not described in the document.
- **Availability/Reliability Assurance**:
  - **High availability**: Implicit via containerized services (e.g., auto-restart on failure) and TimescaleDB’s replication capabilities.
  - **Redundancy**: Critical services (e.g., **MQTT Broker**, **Database**) can be mirrored or clustered (not explicitly detailed but standard practice).
  - **Backup**: TimescaleDB supports periodic snapshots for data recovery.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Digital Twin exemplars (GreenhouseDT and Air Quality Management) utilize an **ad-hoc development approach** rather than relying on commercial digital twin frameworks. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar employs **behavioral adaptation** for both the physical greenhouse system and its digital twin, with **self-adaptive mechanisms** integrated into the design. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar follows a **Model-Driven digital twin engineering** methodology, leveraging **AutomationML** for digital twin representation and **Microsoft Azure tools** for pipeline implementation. Both exemplars rely on **containerization or virtualization technologies** (e.g., virtual machines with pre-recorded data for GreenhouseDT) to facilitate dissemination and reproducibility. **Quality assurance** is implied through the use of **pre-recorded sensor data** (GreenhouseDT) and **structured model-driven pipelines** (Air Quality Management), while **version control** and **evolution management** are inferred from GitHub-hosted repositories with setup instructions. The **Gantry Crane** exemplar (logistics/cyber-physical domain) also adopts a similar **ad-hoc approach**, using **C4 Model** for architecture visualization but lacks explicit lifecycle governance details.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The **Air Quality Management** exemplar provides **three core decision-support applications**: (1) **runtime visualization** of indoor air quality metrics, (2) **simulation** of environmental conditions (e.g., virus spread prevention), and (3) **prediction** of air quality trends. These capabilities rely on **AutomationML-based digital twin models** and **Microsoft Azure tools** for analytics processing. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar lacks explicit decision-making details but implies **self-adaptive behavioral insights** through its **InfluxDB database** (time-series data storage) and **ActiveMQ message broker** (real-time data exchange), enabling **runtime adjustments** to both physical and digital systems. **User interaction** is facilitated via **Raspberry Pi-based sensor integration** (Air Quality) and **virtual machine interfaces** (GreenhouseDT), though no confidence measures or formal decision-making protocols are documented.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The **Air Quality Management** exemplar adheres to **AutomationML** for digital twin modeling, a standardized format for engineering data exchange. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar uses **InfluxDB** (time-series database standard) and **ActiveMQ** (JMS-compliant message broker), while the **Gantry Crane** exemplar employs the **C4 Model** (software architecture standard) for system visualization. No explicit **certification requirements** or **interoperability frameworks** (e.g., OPC UA, FIWARE) are mentioned. The **ad-hoc approach** of these exemplars suggests limited adherence to **domain-specific standards** (e.g., ISO 23247 for digital twins), relying instead on **open-source tools** and **GitHub-hosted repositories** for dissemination.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are

exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give
